questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point source of light is positioned in front of a plane mirror.
options: Only the reflected rays near the normal meet at a point when extended backward.
Light of various colours creates different images.
All the reflected rays meet at a point when extended backward.
Only the reflected rays making a small angle with the mirror meet at a point when extended backward.
questions.solution:
According to the laws of reflection, for a plane mirror, the angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection for all light rays originating from a point source.

When these reflected rays are extended backward, they all intersect at a single point, which forms the virtual image of the point source.

This property holds true for all rays regardless of their angle of incidence or the wavelength of the light.

Therefore, all the reflected rays meet at a point when extended backward.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When studying image formation by spherical mirrors, only paraxial rays are considered because they
options: carry most of the intensity of the incident light
produce nearly a point image from a point source
questions.solution:
Paraxial rays are the rays that are close to the principal axis and make small angles with it.

For a spherical mirror, rays that are far from the principal axis (marginal rays) do not converge at the same point as paraxial rays after reflection, leading to a blurred image. This phenomenon is known as spherical aberration.

By restricting the consideration to paraxial rays, spherical aberration is minimized, and all the reflected rays from a point object converge at a single point.

Thus, paraxial rays produce nearly a point image from a point source.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point object is situated at a distance of $30 \text{ cm}$ from a convex mirror having a focal length of $30 \text{ cm}$. The image will be formed at
questions.solution:
Using the mirror formula, $\dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$

For a convex mirror, focal length $f = +30 \text{ cm}$.

Object distance $u = -30 \text{ cm}$.

Substituting the values, we get:

$\dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{-30} = \dfrac{1}{30}$

$\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{1}{30} + \dfrac{1}{30} = \dfrac{2}{30} = \dfrac{1}{15}$

$v = +15 \text{ cm}$

The positive sign indicates that the image is formed $15 \text{ cm}$ behind the mirror.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, two rays $A$ and $B$ are reflected by a mirror and travel as $A'$ and $B'$. The mirror
questions.solution:
The incident rays $A$ and $B$ are parallel to each other.

After reflection, the corresponding reflected rays $A'$ and $B'$ are also parallel to each other.

For parallel incident rays to remain parallel after reflection, the normals at the points of incidence must be parallel. This is only possible if the reflecting surface is flat.

In the case of spherical mirrors (concave or convex), the normals at different points are not parallel, so a beam of parallel rays will either converge or diverge after reflection.

Therefore, the mirror must be a plane mirror.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three transparent media having refractive indices $\mu_1$, $\mu_2$, and $\mu_3$ are shown in the figure. A point object $O$ is located in the medium $\mu_2$. When the entire medium to the right of the spherical surface has a refractive index $\mu_1$, the image is formed at $O'$. When this entire medium has a refractive index $\mu_3$, the image is formed at $O''$. Under the conditions depicted,
options: the image is formed between $O'$ and $O''$
the image is formed to the left of $O'$
the image is formed to the right of $O''$
two images are formed, one at $O'$ and the other at $O''$
questions.solution:
When light rays from the object $O$ fall on the spherical surface, they are refracted by two different media on the right side.

For the upper half of the spherical surface, the light travels from the medium of refractive index $\mu_2$ to the medium of refractive index $\mu_3$. The refraction formula is given by:
$\dfrac{\mu_3}{v_{upper}} - \dfrac{\mu_2}{u} = \dfrac{\mu_3 - \mu_2}{R}$
This is identical to the case where the entire right medium has a refractive index $\mu_3$. Thus, the rays passing through the upper half will form an image at $O''$.

For the lower half of the spherical surface, the light travels from the medium of refractive index $\mu_2$ to the medium of refractive index $\mu_1$. The refraction formula is given by:
$\dfrac{\mu_1}{v_{lower}} - \dfrac{\mu_2}{u} = \dfrac{\mu_1 - \mu_2}{R}$
This is identical to the case where the entire right medium has a refractive index $\mu_1$. Thus, the rays passing through the lower half will form an image at $O'$.

Since the upper and lower halves of the spherical surface act independently and form images at different locations, two distinct images will be formed, one at $O'$ and the other at $O''$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Total internal reflection can occur only when
options: light travels from an optically rarer medium (lower refractive index) to an optically denser medium
light travels from an optically denser medium to a rarer medium
the refractive indices of the two media are nearly equal
the refractive indices of the two media differ significantly
questions.solution:
Total internal reflection occurs when a light ray travels from an optically denser medium to an optically rarer medium.

According to Snell's law, $\mu_1 \sin i = \mu_2 \sin r$.

For total internal reflection to occur, the angle of refraction $r$ must reach $90^\circ$ for an angle of incidence $i < 90^\circ$.

This implies $\sin i = \dfrac{\mu_2}{\mu_1} \sin 90^\circ = \dfrac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}$.

Since $\sin i < 1$, we must have $\mu_2 < \mu_1$.

Thus, light must travel from a medium of higher refractive index (denser) to a medium of lower refractive index (rarer).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: To account for the effect of the lens thickness $t$, four modifications to the lens formula are proposed. Which of these is most likely correct?
questions.solution:
For a modified lens formula to be correct, it must reduce to the standard thin lens formula when the thickness of the lens approaches zero ($t \to 0$).

The standard thin lens formula is given by:
$\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$

Substituting $t = 0$ into the first expression gives $\dfrac{1}{v - 0} - \dfrac{1}{u + 0} = \dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$, which matches the standard formula.

Substituting $t = 0$ into the second expression gives $\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = 0$, which is incorrect.

Substituting $t = 0$ into the third expression gives $-\dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$, which is incorrect.

Substituting $t = 0$ into the fourth expression gives $\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = 0$, which is incorrect.

Additionally, the fourth expression is dimensionally incorrect as the right-hand side $\dfrac{t}{f}$ is dimensionless, while the left-hand side has dimensions of $[L]^{-1}$.

Therefore, only the first modification is physically consistent.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a double convex lens that has two surfaces of equal radii $R$ and a refractive index $m = 1.5$. The focal length $f$ is given by:
questions.solution:
Using the lens maker's formula:

$\dfrac{1}{f} = (m - 1) \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$

For a double convex lens, the radii of curvature are $R_1 = R$ and $R_2 = -R$.

Given $m = 1.5$:

$\dfrac{1}{f} = (1.5 - 1) \left( \dfrac{1}{R} - \dfrac{1}{-R} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{f} = 0.5 \left( \dfrac{2}{R} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{R}$

$f = R$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point source of light is located at a distance of $2f$ from a converging lens with a focal length of $f$. On the opposite side of the lens, the intensity will be maximum at a distance of
questions.solution:
Using the lens formula, $\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$

Given the object distance $u = -2f$, we have:

$\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{-2f} = \dfrac{1}{f}$

$\dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{2f} = \dfrac{1}{f}$

$\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{2f} = \dfrac{1}{2f}$

$v = 2f$

The rays from the point source converge at the image point, which is at a distance of $2f$ on the opposite side of the lens. Since all the refracted rays converge at this point, the light energy is concentrated in a minimum area. Therefore, the intensity of light will be maximum at a distance of $2f$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel beam of light strikes a converging lens parallel to its principal axis. As a person moves away from the lens along the principal axis on the opposite side, the intensity of the light
questions.solution:
When a parallel beam of light passes through a converging lens, it converges at the principal focus.

The intensity of light is given by the power per unit area, $I = \dfrac{P}{A}$.

As the person moves away from the lens and approaches the focus, the cross-sectional area of the converging beam decreases. This causes the intensity of light to increase, reaching a maximum at the focal point.

As the person moves beyond the focus, the light beam diverges. The cross-sectional area of the beam increases, which causes the intensity to decrease continuously.

Therefore, the intensity of light first increases and then decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A symmetric double convex lens is sliced into two equal parts by a plane perpendicular to its principal axis. Given that the power of the original lens was $4 \text{ D}$, the power of one cut-lens will be
questions.solution:
The power of a symmetric double convex lens is given by $P = (\mu - 1)\left(\dfrac{1}{R} - \dfrac{1}{-R}\right) = \dfrac{2(\mu - 1)}{R}$.

Given $P = 4 \text{ D}$.

When the lens is sliced into two equal parts by a plane perpendicular to its principal axis, each part becomes a plano-convex lens with radii of curvature $R$ and $\infty$.

The power of one such cut lens is $P' = (\mu - 1)\left(\dfrac{1}{R} - \dfrac{1}{\infty}\right) = \dfrac{\mu - 1}{R}$.

Comparing the two expressions, we get $P' = \dfrac{P}{2}$.

Substituting the value of $P$, $P' = \dfrac{4}{2} = 2 \text{ D}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The image produced by a concave mirror
options: is definitely virtual if the object is real
is definitely real if the object is virtual
questions.solution:
Using the mirror formula, $\dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$.

For a concave mirror, the focal length $f$ is negative. Let $f = -F$ where $F > 0$.

If the object is virtual, the object distance $u$ is positive. Let $u = U$ where $U > 0$.

Substituting these into the mirror formula, we get:

$\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{u} = -\dfrac{1}{F} - \dfrac{1}{U} = -\left(\dfrac{1}{F} + \dfrac{1}{U}\right)$

Since both $F$ and $U$ are positive, $\dfrac{1}{v}$ is negative, which implies $v < 0$.

A negative image distance corresponds to a real image formed in front of the mirror. Thus, a concave mirror always forms a real image for a virtual object.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A symmetric double convex lens is divided into two equal halves by a plane containing the principal axis. If the original lens has a power of $4 \text{ D}$, the power of one of the divided parts will be
questions.solution:
The power of a lens is given by the lens maker's formula:

$P = \dfrac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1)\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right)$

When a symmetric double convex lens is cut into two equal halves by a plane containing the principal axis, the radii of curvature $R_1$ and $R_2$ of the refracting surfaces for each half remain exactly the same as those of the original lens.

Since the radii of curvature and the refractive index of the material do not change, the focal length $f$ of each half remains the same as that of the original lens. The only change is that the aperture of the lens is halved, which reduces the intensity of the image but does not affect the focal length.

As the focal length is unchanged, the power of each divided part is equal to the power of the original lens.

Therefore, the power of each part remains $4 \text{ D}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two concave lenses $L_1$ and $L_2$ are placed in contact with one another. If the gap between the two lenses is filled with a substance of smaller refractive index, the magnitude of the focal length of the combination
questions.solution:
When two concave lenses are placed in contact, they touch at their edges, leaving a gap in the middle that is thicker at the center and thinner at the edges. This gap has the shape of a biconvex lens.

Let the powers of the two concave lenses be $P_1$ and $P_2$. Since they are concave, their powers are negative ($P_1 < 0$, $P_2 < 0$).

The initial equivalent power of the combination (with air in the gap) is:
$P_i = P_1 + P_2$

When the gap is filled with a substance of refractive index $\mu > 1$, it acts as a convex lens. Let the power of this liquid lens be $P_3$. Since it is a convex lens, its power is positive ($P_3 > 0$).

The new equivalent power of the combination is:
$P_f = P_1 + P_2 + P_3$

Since $P_1 + P_2$ is negative and $P_3$ is positive, the addition of the positive power reduces the overall magnitude of the negative power of the combination. Thus, $|P_f| < |P_i|$.

The focal length of a lens combination is the reciprocal of its equivalent power ($F = \dfrac{1}{P}$).

Since the magnitude of the equivalent power decreases, the magnitude of the equivalent focal length increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin lens is constructed from a material with a refractive index of $\mu = 1.5$, and both of its sides are convex. When it is immersed in water ($\mu = 1.33$), it will act as
questions.solution:
Using the lens maker's formula:

$\dfrac{1}{f} = \left( \dfrac{\mu_L}{\mu_M} - 1 \right) \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$

For a biconvex lens, $R_1$ is positive and $R_2$ is negative, so the term $\left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$ is positive.

Given the refractive index of the lens $\mu_L = 1.5$ and the refractive index of the surrounding medium (water) $\mu_M = 1.33$.

Since $\mu_L > \mu_M$, the relative refractive index $\dfrac{\mu_L}{\mu_M} > 1$, making the term $\left( \dfrac{\mu_L}{\mu_M} - 1 \right)$ positive.

Thus, the focal length $f$ remains positive.

A lens with a positive focal length acts as a convergent lens. Therefore, the lens continues to act as a convergent lens when immersed in water.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point object $O$ is situated on the principal axis of a convex lens having a focal length $f = 20 \text{ cm}$, at a distance of $40 \text{ cm}$ to its left. The lens has a diameter of $10 \text{ cm}$. An eye is positioned $60 \text{ cm}$ to the right of the lens and at a distance $h$ below the principal axis. The maximum value of $h$ for which the image is visible is
questions.solution:
Using the lens formula to find the position of the image:

$\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$

Substituting $u = -40 \text{ cm}$ and $f = 20 \text{ cm}$:

$\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{-40} = \dfrac{1}{20}$

$\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{1}{20} - \dfrac{1}{40} = \dfrac{1}{40}$

$v = 40 \text{ cm}$

The image is formed at a distance of $40 \text{ cm}$ to the right of the lens. The rays from the object pass through the lens aperture, which has a radius of $R = \dfrac{10}{2} = 5 \text{ cm}$, and converge at the image point.

After converging at the image, the rays diverge. The eye is positioned $60 \text{ cm}$ to the right of the lens, which is at a distance of $60 - 40 = 20 \text{ cm}$ from the image.

Let the radius of the diverging beam at the position of the eye be $r$. Using similar triangles formed by the image point, the lens aperture, and the cross-section of the beam at the eye:

$\dfrac{r}{20} = \dfrac{5}{40}$

$r = 20 \times \dfrac{5}{40} = 2.5 \text{ cm}$

For the image to be visible, the eye must be within this diverging beam of light. Therefore, the maximum distance $h$ below the principal axis is $2.5 \text{ cm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A convex lens is fabricated from a material possessing a refractive index of $1.2$. Both of its surfaces are convex. If immersed in water ($\mu = 1.33$), it will function as
questions.solution:
According to the lens maker's formula:

$\dfrac{1}{f} = \left( \dfrac{\mu_L}{\mu_M} - 1 \right) \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$

For a biconvex lens, $R_1$ is positive and $R_2$ is negative, so $\left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right) > 0$.

Given $\mu_L = 1.2$ and $\mu_M = 1.33$.

Since $\mu_L < \mu_M$, the term $\left( \dfrac{\mu_L}{\mu_M} - 1 \right)$ is negative.

Therefore, the focal length $f$ becomes negative.

A lens with a negative focal length behaves as a divergent lens.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The defect where rays of different colours fail to converge at a single point after passing through a converging lens is termed
questions.solution:
The refractive index of the material of a lens varies with the wavelength (colour) of light. Since the focal length of a lens depends on the refractive index, rays of different colours have different focal lengths and fail to converge at a single point after passing through the lens.

This defect is known as chromatic aberration.
